cards.front: What is 'responsibility layering' and how does it prevent pattern stacking in the enterprise stack?
cards.back: Responsibility layering assigns one pattern per one design pressure — no two patterns own the same concern. When building the enterprise stack, you check: does a pattern here address a pressure not yet owned by any other pattern? If two patterns solve the same pressure, one is redundant. Layering is coherence; stacking is accumulation without assignment.

cards.front: A system has Service Layer + Repository + Unit of Work, but no real domain behavior — all rules live in the service methods. What is wrong and what is the fix?
cards.back: This is Anemic Domain Model — the enterprise stack is structurally present but Domain Model is missing or hollow. Fix: either enrich domain objects with the business rules currently in services (if domain complexity justifies it), or remove the Domain Model layer and acknowledge this is a Transaction Script system with persistence infrastructure.

cards.front: How do Command, Observer, and State compose in a text editor to form a coherent pattern stack?
cards.back: Command: every edit action is a command — enabling undo/redo history. Observer: the document notifies the UI (and other components) when content changes — decoupled rendering. State: the editor transitions between modes (insert, select, read-only) and delegates behavior to the current state object. Each pattern owns one pressure; no overlap.

cards.front: Distinguish an editor/workflow pattern stack from a backend persistence stack — what forces differ?
cards.back: Editor/workflow stack: Command (action lifecycle), Observer (reactive UI updates), State (mode switching) — forces are around time, user interaction, and behavioral transitions. Backend persistence stack: Service Layer, Domain Model, Data Mapper, Repository, UoW — forces are around orchestration, invariants, O-R mismatch, query boundaries, and transaction scope. Different forces → different patterns.

cards.front: What is accidental complexity in a system with too many patterns, and how do you detect it?
cards.back: Accidental complexity is difficulty introduced by the design itself (not by the problem). Detect it: new developers need to understand 4+ pattern layers before making a simple change; abstractions exist without a traceable pressure; removing a layer doesn't break any behavior. If patterns can be removed without cost, they were accidental structure.

cards.front: What is the composition heuristic for adding patterns to a growing codebase?
cards.back: Add one pattern only when a concrete pressure appears that no existing pattern already owns. The sequence: (1) Start with the simplest design that works. (2) When a specific pressure (coupling, volatility, coordination, mismatch) makes the simple design costly, introduce the one pattern that relieves it. (3) Repeat only when the next pressure appears. Never preemptively.

cards.front: Distinguish 'coherent pattern stack' from 'pattern dump'.
cards.back: Coherent stack: each pattern is traceable to a specific pressure it relieves; removing any one would reintroduce that pressure. Pattern dump: patterns are present because they seemed appropriate or fashionable; multiple patterns share overlapping concerns; removing several would have no observable impact on the system's behavior.

cards.front: A naive design starts as a single Python function. Sketch the pressure-triggered pattern additions as the system grows.
cards.back: Step 0: one function. → Coupling pressure (DB hardcoded): add DI. → Query scattering: add Repository. → Multi-table writes: add Unit of Work. → Shared business rules across scripts: add Domain Model. → Different runtime environments: add Plugin. Each addition is triggered by a specific cost, not planned upfront. The stack is coherent because every layer has a traceable origin.

cards.front: What is research gap G005 in this course's evidence base?
cards.back: Missing GoF primary source — the course's research did not have direct access to the original Design Patterns (GoF) text. Claims about original GoF intent rely on secondary sources and widely cited summaries. This limits confidence about what the GoF authors actually intended versus how their patterns have been interpreted over time.

cards.front: What is research gap G006 in this course's evidence base?
cards.back: Context-dependent indirection threshold — no empirical study measures at what team size, codebase size, or domain complexity a specific pattern starts paying rent. The thresholds given in this course are synthesized from practitioner sources, not experimental data. They are good heuristics but not validated rules.

cards.front: What is research gap G007 in this course's evidence base?
cards.back: Thinner editor and interactive system case studies — the research base is richer for backend/enterprise patterns (Fowler's PoEAA territory) than for editor, game, or UI interaction patterns. Claims about Command/Observer/State composition in interactive systems are supported by fewer sources and should be held with lower confidence.

cards.front: How should a learner calibrate confidence when applying this course's pattern thresholds, given G006?
cards.back: Treat all pressure thresholds as practitioner heuristics, not validated rules. Apply them as a starting check, not a definitive gate. When in doubt, start without the pattern and add it when concrete costs appear — this strategy is safe under uncertainty about where the threshold is. Research cannot tell you the exact number; your codebase feedback can.

cards.front: What evidence would resolve gap G006 (indirection threshold) and make it a settled claim?
cards.back: Controlled studies or large-scale retrospective analyses measuring: (a) codebase change frequency before vs. after adding a pattern, (b) bug rates in pattern-absent vs. pattern-present codebases at matched complexity levels, (c) developer onboarding time as a function of pattern layer count. Currently no such data exists in the literature surveyed.

questions.question: A developer notices that three different services all construct their own database connections inside their constructors. Which design pressure category best describes the root cause?

options.A: Coordination pressure — the services need to share state
options.B: Construction coupling — objects manage their own dependency acquisition
options.C: Boundary mismatch — the database schema doesn't match the domain model
options.D: Volatility pressure — the database changes frequently

questions.explanation: Construction coupling occurs when objects reach out and acquire their own dependencies rather than receiving them. This is not a schema mismatch (C), shared state issue (A), or change-frequency problem (D).

questions.question: Which statement best captures the pressure-first design principle?

options.A: Always select a pattern from the GoF catalog before writing any code
options.B: Identify and confirm a recurring design force before introducing any structural response
options.C: Refactor code once it exceeds 200 lines, regardless of symptom
options.D: Apply patterns preemptively to avoid future pain

questions.explanation: Pressure-first means confirming a recurring force exists before adding structure. Speculative or line-count-based application (A, C, D) leads to abstraction debt.

questions.question: A team argues whether to add an Adapter before a third-party library exists. What is the pressure-first question they should ask?

options.A: How many lines of code will the Adapter save?
options.B: Is the boundary mismatch real and present, or only imagined?
options.C: Does the team know the Adapter pattern well enough to implement it?
options.D: Has the library been used in other projects before?

questions.explanation: The pressure-first check is whether the force (boundary mismatch) is present now and recurring. Team skill (C) and prior usage (D) are irrelevant to whether the pressure is real. Line counts (A) miss the point entirely.

questions.question: What distinguishes a pattern language from a pattern catalog?

options.A: A catalog is more comprehensive; a pattern language only covers common patterns
options.B: A pattern language includes UML diagrams; a catalog does not
options.C: A pattern language shows how patterns relate and compose; a catalog lists them independently
options.D: A catalog is used by beginners; a pattern language is for experts

questions.explanation: A pattern language is a vocabulary of related moves with compositional grammar — patterns know their natural neighbors. A catalog is an independent list without relational structure, encouraging pick-and-apply rather than composition.

questions.question: A codebase uses Service Layer + Repository + Unit of Work. A new developer says this is 'over-engineered pattern stacking.' What is the correct counter-argument?

options.A: All three are required by enterprise frameworks so they must be kept
options.B: Each pattern addresses a distinct pressure; removing any one reintroduces that pressure
options.C: The patterns came from the same book, so they naturally belong together
options.D: Pattern stacking is fine as long as each pattern is well-documented

questions.explanation: Coherent composition means each pattern owns a distinct force. If removing a layer reintroduces a real cost, the layer is justified. The origins of the patterns (C) or their documentation (D) are irrelevant to whether they earn their presence.

questions.question: Which of the five pressure categories best describes the situation where a class has ten if-elif branches checking deployment environment?

options.A: Coordination pressure
options.B: Boundary mismatch
options.C: Volatility pressure
options.D: Coupling pressure

questions.explanation: Volatility pressure arises when configuration or environment-specific variation is scattered rather than isolated. The class changes whenever a new environment is added — that's volatility, not coordination (A), mismatch (B), coupling (D), or duplication (E).

questions.question: What is the primary risk of skipping tradeoff triage after identifying a real design pressure?

options.A: You may select the wrong pressure category
options.B: The pattern you choose may cost more indirection than the pressure it relieves
options.C: The pattern may not have a name in the GoF catalog
options.D: Other developers may not recognize the pattern

questions.explanation: Tradeoff triage compares what a structural response costs (indirection, new abstractions, onboarding) against what it relieves. Skipping it risks choosing a pattern whose overhead exceeds the problem's cost. Naming concerns (C, D) and pressure classification (A) are separate from this economic check.

questions.question: Explain the difference between a 'symptom' and a 'pressure' in pressure-first design diagnostics. Give one example of each.

required_points: Symptom is an observable problem (slow tests, hard-to-change code, duplicated logic)
Pressure is the underlying recurring force causing the symptom (coupling, volatility, coordination failure, boundary mismatch)
At least one concrete example of a symptom
At least one concrete example of the underlying pressure behind it
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Examples can vary; the key is the symptom-to-pressure causal relationship
answer_key.full_credit: Clear distinction, correct causal relationship, concrete examples for both
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct distinction without examples, or examples without the causal link explained

questions.question: What is 'coherent composition' and how is it different from 'pattern stacking'? Give one example of a coherent composition.

required_points: Coherent composition: each pattern addresses a distinct, traceable pressure; every layer is justified
Pattern stacking: patterns accumulate without pressure assignment; some patterns solve the same concern or exist with no force behind them
Example must name at least two patterns and identify the different pressures each addresses (e.g., Repository = query abstraction; Unit of Work = transaction coordination)
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Any valid combination of two patterns with distinct pressures is acceptable
answer_key.full_credit: Clear distinction, correct characterization of both, concrete example with pressure mapping
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct distinction without example, or example without pressure mapping

questions.question: A teammate proposes adding a Registry to 'make services easier to find.' What pressure-first questions would you ask before agreeing?

required_points: Is the difficulty of finding services causing a real, recurring cost now?
Is explicit injection (passing dependencies) not feasible due to call-tree depth or framework constraints?
Does the convenience of a Registry outweigh the hidden-dependency cost it introduces?
Is there more than one place where this lookup difficulty actually exists?
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Questions can be phrased differently; must address present reality vs. speculation, and the visibility tradeoff
answer_key.full_credit: At least two substantive pressure-first checks with a tradeoff consideration
answer_key.partial_credit: One check without the tradeoff dimension

questions.prompt: A developer joins a codebase and finds five patterns they can name by GoF category. They conclude the system is 'well-designed.' Another developer says none of those patterns are paying rent. Using pressure-first reasoning and the concept of coherent composition, explain what evidence each developer should gather to settle the debate.
questions.guidance: A strong response explains what 'paying rent' means operationally, identifies the evidence needed to confirm each pattern is relieving a present pressure, and explains how coherent composition differs from pattern presence. Bonus: discusses what removing one layer would reveal.

rubric.A: Defines rent payment operationally, maps each pattern to a pressure question, explains coherent composition vs. presence, and uses at least one concrete example of how to test whether a pattern is justified
rubric.B: Defines rent payment, discusses pressure-pattern relationship, limited on evidence methodology or composition distinction
rubric.C: Names the relevant concepts (pressure, rent) without operational definition or evidence methodology

questions.question: A `ReportService` opens a new database connection in its `__init__` method. What construction pressure is present and what does dependency injection resolve?

options.A: Volatility pressure; DI removes the need for a database entirely
options.B: Construction coupling; DI moves dependency acquisition to an external caller
options.C: Coordination pressure; DI ensures all services share the same connection
options.D: Boundary mismatch; DI normalizes the database interface

questions.explanation: The object is acquiring its own dependency — construction coupling. DI resolves this by pushing the dependency in from outside rather than letting the object pull it. DI doesn't remove databases (A), fix coordination (C), or normalize interfaces (D).

questions.question: What is the 'composition root' and why should there be exactly one in a DI-based system?

options.A: The base class all services inherit from; one ensures a consistent interface
options.B: The single place where all real implementations are assembled and wired; one prevents environment-specific decisions from scattering
options.C: The main function that starts the application; one per process is a technical requirement
options.D: The configuration file that lists all dependencies; one prevents duplication

questions.explanation: The composition root is the one place where the specific implementations are wired together. Scattering configuration logic across many classes reintroduces construction coupling even when DI interfaces are used. A, C, D describe adjacent but different concepts.

questions.question: What runtime pressure does the Plugin pattern address that DI alone does not?

options.A: Plugin removes the need for interfaces by using direct instantiation
options.B: Plugin centralizes environment-based implementation selection that would otherwise scatter as conditionals
options.C: Plugin eliminates the need for a composition root by selecting dependencies automatically
options.D: Plugin handles thread safety in multi-environment deployments

questions.explanation: Plugin's distinct value is confining 'which implementation for this environment' to one extension boundary. DI handles construction wiring but does not prevent environment selection logic from leaking into many classes. A, C, D are incorrect characterizations.

questions.question: How does Plugin differ from Strategy as a variation mechanism?

options.A: Plugin selects behavior at runtime based on application logic; Strategy selects at startup from config
options.B: Plugin selects implementation at deployment/startup from external config; Strategy selects algorithm at runtime based on logic
options.C: Plugin and Strategy are synonymous; Strategy is the GoF name for the same pattern
options.D: Plugin applies only to external libraries; Strategy applies to internal logic

questions.explanation: Plugin is deployment-time variation (environment config determines which implementation loads). Strategy is runtime behavioral variation (logic within the running application selects the algorithm). A has them reversed. C and D are incorrect.

questions.question: When does the Prototype pattern earn its keep over direct construction?

options.A: When you have more than three subclasses to instantiate
options.B: When an object's complex configured state is more expensive to rebuild than to clone
options.C: When the language does not support constructors with multiple parameters
options.D: When objects must be created in parallel across multiple threads

questions.explanation: Prototype is justified when constructing from scratch is expensive and the configured state of an existing instance is the valuable part. Subclass count (A), language features (C), and threading (D) are not the relevant pressure.

questions.question: Claim C2 states creational patterns can be over-applied. Which scenario is the clearest example of this?

options.A: Using DI when a service creates its own database connection in production
options.B: Adding a Plugin architecture for a payment service that currently has one implementation and no second planned
options.C: Using Repository to centralize SQL queries scattered across 12 service methods
options.D: Adding Unit of Work when a service modifies 6 objects in a single transaction

questions.explanation: Adding Plugin infrastructure before any second implementation exists or is concretely planned is the clearest case of paying indirection cost before pressure is real. A, C, D all have clear present pressures justifying the patterns.

questions.question: Contradiction 2 in this course says classic pattern forms may not endure even when the underlying problem does. Which example best illustrates this?

options.A: The Service Layer pattern is no longer needed because microservices handle orchestration
options.B: Observer's GoF class hierarchy is often unnecessary in languages with first-class functions, yet the fan-out notification problem persists
options.C: Singleton is outdated because modern frameworks always use DI containers
options.D: Data Mapper is obsolete now that ORMs handle object-relational translation automatically

questions.explanation: Contradiction 2 is specifically about form vs. problem: the notification fan-out problem persists, but modern languages can solve it with callbacks/event emitters without a ConcreteObserver class hierarchy. A, C, D either overstate obsolescence or misidentify the pattern.

questions.question: A service has `if env == 'prod': mailer = SMTPMailer(); elif env == 'staging': mailer = FakeMailer()` in 8 different methods. Explain which pattern addresses this and why DI alone is insufficient.

required_points: Pattern: Plugin (or plugin-style extension boundary)
DI addresses construction coupling — the object receives the dependency rather than constructing it
But DI does not prevent the env selection logic from repeating in 8 places — it only moves construction
Plugin confines the env-based selection to one extension boundary; the service methods become unaware of which mailer was selected
answer_key.acceptable_variations: May phrase Plugin as 'a factory that reads from config at startup' — that is the plugin mechanism
answer_key.full_credit: Names Plugin, explains DI limitation clearly, explains Plugin's unique value (single selection point)
answer_key.partial_credit: Correctly identifies the problem with DI or the role of Plugin but not both

questions.question: Distinguish Dependency Injection from the Service Locator pattern at the point of dependency acquisition. Why does the distinction matter for testability?

required_points: DI: dependency is pushed in at construction time by an external caller — explicit, visible in signature
Service Locator: object pulls the dependency from a shared registry at runtime — implicit, hidden inside implementation
Testability: DI allows a test to pass a mock/fake via the constructor without any setup of a registry; SL requires tests to configure the registry before the object under test can run
answer_key.acceptable_variations: May phrase the push/pull distinction differently
answer_key.full_credit: Correct push/pull distinction, visibility difference, and testability implication
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct push/pull without testability, or correct testability implication without explaining why

questions.prompt: A team is building a data processing pipeline that needs to support three execution environments: local development, CI test, and production cloud. They are debating DI, Plugin, and explicit parameter passing. Using the concepts from Module 2, explain how each mechanism addresses the construction/runtime variation pressure differently, when each is most appropriate, and which combination you would recommend for this scenario.
questions.guidance: A strong response maps each mechanism to its pressure: explicit passing (simple, no container), DI (construction coupling when call trees are shallow), Plugin (deployment-time environment selection when multiple implementations exist). A strong answer recommends a specific combination with reasoning and acknowledges the tradeoffs.

rubric.A: Correctly maps all three mechanisms to their pressures, recommends Plugin + DI with clear reasoning about the environment selection problem, acknowledges composition root and tradeoffs
rubric.B: Correctly maps two of three, makes a justified recommendation with partial reasoning
rubric.C: Names all three mechanisms without pressure mapping or recommendation justification

M03.title: Assessment: Ambient Access and Hidden Dependencies

questions.question: A Registry provides convenience by allowing any object to look up services by name. What is the primary design cost introduced by this convenience?

options.A: It creates construction coupling because objects must inherit from the registry
options.B: It makes dependencies implicit — callers cannot see what an object depends on without reading its internals
options.C: It introduces a boundary mismatch between the domain model and the registry schema
options.D: It prevents unit testing because registries require real implementations

questions.explanation: The fundamental Registry cost is hidden dependencies — the dependency graph is invisible from the outside. A is wrong (no inheritance required). C conflates Registry with ORM concerns. D is partly true but the root design cost is the visibility problem, not testability per se.

questions.question: Under what narrow condition is a Registry legitimately justified over explicit dependency injection?

options.A: When the team prefers not to refactor the composition root
options.B: When the dependency is genuinely global in scope and passing it through many layers would create significant overhead
options.C: Whenever more than five services need access to the same object
options.D: When the dependency changes at runtime and must be hot-swapped

questions.explanation: The narrow justification is: genuinely global scope + measurably high injection overhead. Team preference (A) and service count (C) are not design pressures. Hot-swapping (D) is a runtime variation concern addressed by Strategy or Plugin, not Registry.

questions.question: Service Locator is sometimes called a legitimate tool. In which context is its use most defensible?

options.A: Any application where the team wants to avoid writing a composition root
options.B: A framework or plugin host where the caller does not control object construction
options.C: Large codebases where there are more than 50 injectable services
options.D: Systems where all services have the same lifecycle (request-scoped)

questions.explanation: Service Locator is most defensible when the caller genuinely cannot receive the dependency by injection — e.g., a framework instantiates the object and the developer has no access to the constructor call. The other options describe situations where DI is typically still viable.

questions.question: Contradiction 1 states the threshold between acceptable Service Locator and architectural debt is unresolved. What makes this particular contradiction unresolvable by the research reviewed in this course?

options.A: Service Locator was deprecated after GoF published their book
options.B: The threshold depends on context factors (team size, framework, call-tree depth) that no study has empirically measured
options.C: Fowler and Nystrom use different definitions of Service Locator, making comparison impossible
options.D: There are no real-world codebases that use Service Locator anymore

questions.explanation: The research surveyed by this course found no empirical measurement of the point where SL transitions from acceptable convenience to architectural debt. The contextual factors vary too much for a universal rule. A, C, D are factually incorrect.

questions.question: What is the most concrete indicator that a Singleton is being used as ambient global state rather than as a legitimate single-instance controller?

options.A: The singleton class has more than one method
options.B: The singleton is referenced in more than 10 places
options.C: Test isolation breaks — state leaks from one test to the next unless explicitly reset
options.D: The singleton was created before DI containers were available

questions.explanation: State leaking between tests is the most concrete operational signal that the singleton is carrying global mutable state rather than controlling a genuine single-instance lifecycle. Reference count (B) and method count (A) are not indicators. Historical origin (D) is irrelevant.

questions.question: Distinguish a legitimate Singleton from Singleton-as-global-access-point.

options.A: Legitimate Singleton is thread-safe; global access point is not
options.B: Legitimate Singleton enforces a genuine single-instance domain requirement; global access point exists only for convenience of avoiding injection
options.C: Legitimate Singleton is created at startup; global access point is created lazily
options.D: Legitimate Singleton implements an interface; global access point returns a concrete class

questions.explanation: The distinction is whether the 'single instance' constraint is a genuine domain requirement (e.g., one printer spooler) or just a convenience for avoiding DI overhead. Thread safety (A) and creation timing (C) are implementation details, not the distinguishing criterion. Interface usage (D) is unrelated.

questions.question: Claim C5 identifies the central failure mode for patterns as applying them before real pressure exists. Which of the following best applies this claim to the Singleton pattern?

options.A: Using Singleton for a configuration store that is genuinely read-only and process-global
options.B: Using Singleton for every service class to ensure one instance per application
options.C: Using Singleton to enforce that a connection pool has exactly one instance managing thread-safe access
options.D: Using Singleton for a hardware interface driver where one physical device must map to one software object

questions.explanation: Applying Singleton to every service 'for consistency' is using it before any single-instance pressure is real — it's cargo-cult architecture. A, C, and D all have genuine single-instance requirements that justify the pattern.

questions.question: Explain why dependency visibility is described as a 'first-class design concern' in this module. Give one example where low visibility created a concrete problem.

required_points: Dependency visibility means the set of things an object depends on is declared and visible to callers — not hidden inside the implementation
Low visibility means callers cannot reason about what an object needs without reading its source, which makes testing, debugging, and refactoring harder
Concrete example: e.g., an object using a Registry internally — a test can call the method without knowing it needs a DB connection until a runtime error occurs
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Example can involve Singleton or Service Locator as the hidden-dependency mechanism
answer_key.full_credit: Defines visibility, explains why it matters, gives a concrete failure-mode example
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct definition or correct example but not both with the causal link

questions.question: Describe the test isolation problem caused by Singleton global state and explain one way to resolve it without eliminating the Singleton.

required_points: Problem: singleton carries mutable state across test boundaries — test A modifies the singleton and test B inherits that modification, making tests order-dependent

Resolution without elimination: provide a reset/clear method that tests call in setUp/tearDown, OR scope the singleton to the test context, OR use a DI approach that injects the singleton as an interface (tests inject a fresh instance)
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Any valid test isolation strategy that addresses singleton state leakage
answer_key.full_credit: Correct diagnosis of the test-isolation failure mode and one concrete resolution strategy
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct diagnosis without a resolution strategy

questions.prompt: A senior developer on your team argues: 'Service Locator, Registry, and Singleton are all just convenient global access — they're all bad and should be eliminated.' A junior developer argues they're all perfectly fine and easier than DI. Using Contradiction 1 and the ambient access analysis from Module 3, write a nuanced position that neither fully agrees with either developer.
questions.guidance: A strong response acknowledges the legitimate use cases for all three (framework constraints, genuine single-instance requirements, genuinely global infrastructure objects) while explaining their shared visibility cost and the specific conditions that justify them. It should cite the unresolved threshold in Contradiction 1 and avoid universal rules in either direction.

rubric.A: Acknowledges legitimate use cases for all three, articulates the shared visibility cost, cites the unresolved threshold, gives specific contextual factors that shift the judgment
rubric.B: Correct on the visibility cost and at least one legitimate use case, limited on contextual nuance or Contradiction 1
rubric.C: Picks a side (agrees with senior or junior) without contextual differentiation

questions.question: What is the orchestration pressure that justifies a Service Layer?

options.A: Multiple domain objects share a single database table
options.B: The same use case sequence is needed in multiple entry points and would otherwise scatter
options.C: The application has too many classes for a single developer to understand
options.D: Domain objects are too simple to hold any behavior

questions.explanation: Service Layer is justified when the same multi-step operation (validate, load, check, update, notify) must be coordinated consistently across multiple entry points. A is a persistence concern, C is a complexity concern unrelated to patterns, and D describes a symptom of anemic domain model, not a Service Layer justification.

questions.question: Where does Service Layer stop, and what is the failure mode when it expands beyond its boundary?

options.A: It stops at the database; failure mode is too many SQL queries
options.B: It stops at orchestration; failure mode is absorbing domain rules and creating an anemic domain model
options.C: It stops at the UI; failure mode is coupling to presentation concerns
options.D: It stops when it reaches 500 lines; failure mode is a God class

questions.explanation: Service Layer owns orchestration (sequencing, coordination). Business rules belong in domain objects. When Service Layer absorbs rules, domain objects become data bags — the anemic domain model. A, C, D describe different but incorrect boundaries.

questions.question: A method checks `if user is None: return 'Guest'` in 15 separate places. What does the Special Case pattern replace these checks with?

options.A: A centralized null check utility function called once
options.B: A GuestUser object that implements the same interface as User with default behavior
options.C: A database default value that prevents null users from being created
options.D: A validation layer that rejects null inputs before they reach the method

questions.explanation: Special Case replaces repeated null checks with a polymorphic object (GuestUser) that provides correct default behavior through the same interface — callers no longer branch. A centralizes but doesn't eliminate the check. C and D prevent the case from arising rather than handling it with the pattern.

questions.question: What edge-case pressure justifies Special Case (Null Object) over a simple null check?

options.A: The null check logic is complex and involves more than one condition
options.B: The same edge condition is checked in many places and each check must be kept consistent
options.C: The team wants to avoid using null values in Python
options.D: The object being checked is an external dependency

questions.explanation: The pressure is scattered, repeated edge-case handling that must stay consistent. When the same null/sentinel check appears in many places, any policy change requires touching all of them. Special Case localizes the policy once. A describes complexity, not recurrence. C is a style preference. D is a boundary issue, not a Special Case trigger.

questions.question: Claim C3 states structural patterns are most valuable at unstable or mismatched boundaries. Which of the following best applies this claim?

options.A: Adding a facade around a stable, well-documented internal library the team controls
options.B: Adding an adapter at the boundary between the domain and a third-party API whose response format changes with each version
options.C: Using a service layer to reduce the number of classes in the codebase
options.D: Applying data mapper to a project with a single SQLite table and no domain behavior

questions.explanation: C3 is about boundary instability and mismatch. A third-party API with changing response formats is the clearest example of an unstable, mismatched boundary where structural isolation pays. A describes a stable boundary. C is not a structural-pattern justification. D applies data mapper before domain complexity warrants it.

questions.question: A system has both orchestration pressure (repeated use-case sequences) and edge-condition pressure (null user in 10 places). How should these two pressures be addressed?

options.A: Pick the most pressing one and use one pattern for both
options.B: Address each with its own pattern: Service Layer for orchestration, Special Case for edge conditions
options.C: Use Service Layer and add null-handling logic inside the service methods
options.D: Use Special Case for everything and skip the Service Layer

questions.explanation: Coherent composition addresses each distinct pressure with its own pattern. Service Layer owns orchestration; Special Case owns the null policy. Merging them (A, C) or picking one (D) leaves one pressure unaddressed or mixes concerns.

questions.question: How do you decide between Service Layer and Registry when both seem like potential responses to an access boundary problem?

options.A: Service Layer for problems with more than three steps; Registry for simpler problems
options.B: Service Layer for orchestrating a sequence of operations; Registry for locating a shared object
options.C: Registry is always preferred because it requires less code
options.D: Service Layer is always preferred because it provides better testability

questions.explanation: The patterns address different pressures: Service Layer = use-case coordination (sequencing operations), Registry = lookup (finding a shared object without threading it through call trees). Pressure maps to pattern. A uses complexity as a proxy — incorrect. C and D are blanket preferences, not pressure-based reasoning.

questions.question: Explain why Special Case (Null Object) is described as 'localizing policy' and null checks are described as 'scattering policy.' Give an example showing the difference.

required_points: Null check: each call site is responsible for deciding what to do with a null — the policy lives at the point of use and can drift between sites
Special Case: the policy is inside the GuestUser (or similar) object — it is called once when the degenerate object is created and applied consistently wherever the object is used
Example: user.display_name() returns 'Guest' from GuestUser without the caller checking for null — the policy is local to the object, not scattered across callers
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Any domain example showing the localization
answer_key.full_credit: Correct policy-localization vs. policy-scattering distinction with concrete example
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct principle without example, or example without the localization explanation

questions.question: A Service Layer method contains this logic: `if order.total > 1000 and order.customer.tier == 'gold': apply_discount(order, 0.15)`. Should this stay in the service or move to the domain? Justify your answer using what you know about where business rules belong.

required_points: This is a business rule (discount eligibility based on order total and customer tier) — it belongs in the domain
Service Layer should orchestrate (call domain.apply_discount_if_eligible()) not contain the rule
If the rule stays in the service, any other workflow needing the same discount logic must duplicate it or call this service — violating encapsulation
Domain location: e.g., Order.apply_eligible_discount() or a domain service that knows both Order and Customer
answer_key.acceptable_variations: May place the rule on Order or on a domain service — either is defensible
answer_key.full_credit: Correctly identifies the rule as domain logic, explains why it should move, and names a domain location
answer_key.partial_credit: Correctly identifies it should move without explaining why or naming a destination

questions.prompt: A team is building a customer portal. Users can be logged-in, guests, and suspended accounts. The portal has 6 different features that behave differently for each user type. The team is considering three approaches: (1) null checks everywhere, (2) a single UserType enum with switch statements, (3) Special Case objects for Guest and Suspended. Using pressure-first reasoning and boundary-isolation principles from Module 4, evaluate all three approaches and recommend one.
questions.guidance: A strong response maps the edge-condition pressure to each approach: approach 1 scatters policy, approach 2 centralizes the type but still requires each feature to know all cases, approach 3 localizes each user type's policy in an object. The recommendation should explain what happens when a fourth user type is added (extensibility test).

rubric.A: Evaluates all three approaches with pressure mapping, identifies Special Case as the recommendation, justifies with the extensibility test and policy-localization argument
rubric.B: Evaluates two of three correctly, makes a justified recommendation with some reasoning
rubric.C: Recommends one approach without comparative evaluation of the alternatives

questions.question: What object-relational mismatch does Data Mapper address that Active Record does not?

options.A: Data Mapper supports more database vendors than Active Record
options.B: Data Mapper keeps domain objects ignorant of the database schema; Active Record mixes persistence knowledge into the domain object
options.C: Data Mapper is faster than Active Record for write-heavy workloads
options.D: Data Mapper eliminates the need for SQL; Active Record requires raw SQL

questions.explanation: The core distinction is domain/persistence separation. Active Record objects know how to save themselves — persistence leaks into the domain. Data Mapper keeps them separate — the domain model has no save() method. A, C, D describe performance or vendor concerns unrelated to the mismatch addressed.

questions.question: Under what domain complexity threshold does Data Mapper become worth its additional layer?

options.A: Any application with more than one database table
options.B: Any application using PostgreSQL rather than SQLite
options.C: When domain objects have meaningful invariants and behavior that would be corrupted by mixing in persistence concerns
options.D: When the development team has more than three engineers

questions.explanation: Data Mapper earns its layer when domain complexity is high enough that mixing persistence into domain objects corrupts the model. For simple CRUD with no meaningful domain behavior, Active Record is cheaper and sufficient. A, B, D apply irrelevant proxies (table count, DB type, team size).

questions.question: How does Repository differ from a DAO (Data Access Object) in how it presents its interface to callers?

options.A: Repository supports transactions; DAO does not
options.B: Repository presents a collection-like, domain-facing interface; DAO presents a table-close, data-structure interface
options.C: Repository is only used with NoSQL databases; DAO is used with relational databases
options.D: Repository generates SQL at runtime; DAO uses stored procedures

questions.explanation: Repository's key characteristic is the collection metaphor — callers think in domain terms (find_active_subscribers()). DAO is thinner and closer to the database structure. A, C, D describe implementation details unrelated to the abstraction level distinction.

questions.question: When does adding a Repository become needless indirection rather than a justified abstraction?

options.A: When the application has fewer than 10 domain objects
options.B: When the repository methods are pass-throughs to ORM calls with no domain logic or reuse value
options.C: When the team uses an ORM that was released more than five years ago
options.D: When queries are fast and do not require optimization

questions.explanation: If every repository method simply delegates to an ORM call (repo.get(id) → Model.objects.get(id=id)) with no domain abstraction added, the Repository layer has no abstraction value. The ORM already provides the collection interface. A, C, D are not relevant decision factors.

questions.question: What transaction-scope problem does Unit of Work solve that explicit .save() calls on each object do not?

options.A: UoW makes write operations faster by batching them
options.B: UoW prevents the service from needing to know which objects it loaded
options.C: UoW coordinates all multi-object changes into one atomic commit, preventing partial writes and forgotten saves
options.D: UoW automatically creates rollback scripts in case of failure

questions.explanation: UoW's core value is atomic multi-object commit — tracking all dirty objects and writing them in one transaction. Explicit saves require the service to know and call save on each object, risking partial writes. A is a possible side effect, not the core value. B is partially true but incomplete. D describes database features, not UoW.

questions.question: How do Repository and Unit of Work complement each other at the persistence boundary?

options.A: Repository handles reads; Unit of Work handles writes for the same table
options.B: Repository provides domain-facing query access; Unit of Work coordinates atomic write-back for all objects loaded through repositories
options.C: Repository replaces Unit of Work when using modern ORMs
options.D: Unit of Work is only needed when Repository is not present

questions.explanation: They address different pressures in collaboration: Repository = where to get/find domain objects (query abstraction). UoW = how all changes commit atomically (transaction coordination). A incorrectly implies they handle reads and writes for the same table separately. C and D suggest mutual exclusivity that is wrong.

questions.question: A service modifies an Order and 4 LineItems, then calls order.save(), item1.save(), item2.save(), item3.save(), item4.save(). A network error occurs after item3.save(). What does Unit of Work prevent in this scenario?

options.A: It prevents the network error from occurring
options.B: It prevents the partial write — either all 5 objects commit together or none do
options.C: It logs the error and retries item4.save() automatically
options.D: It splits the saves into two separate transactions to reduce risk

questions.explanation: UoW wraps all changes in one atomic transaction. A partial write (3 of 5 objects saved) leaves data inconsistent — UoW prevents this by ensuring all-or-nothing commit semantics. A, C, D describe error handling or optimization strategies, not UoW's transactional guarantee.

questions.question: Explain the 'persistence boundary' and why it matters for domain object design. What does it mean for a domain object to be 'DB-ignorant'?

required_points: Persistence boundary: the layer that separates domain objects from relational database concerns (SQL, table structure, foreign keys)
DB-ignorant domain object: has no save(), find(), or SQL methods — it only contains domain behavior and state
Matters because: mixing persistence into domain objects ties business logic to database structure, making both harder to change and test independently
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Can express the same idea using Data Mapper or Active Record as contrast examples
answer_key.full_credit: Defines the boundary, explains DB-ignorance, explains why it matters with a testability or change-isolation consequence
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct definition without the 'why it matters' explanation

questions.question: A UserService has 15 different SQL query strings embedded in its methods. Describe the Repository refactor: what changes, what stays the same, and what pressure is addressed.

required_points: What changes: the 15 SQL strings move into a UserRepository with named domain-facing methods (find_by_email, find_active_users, etc.)
What stays the same: the UserService still calls the same logical operations — it just calls user_repo.find_by_email() instead of writing SQL
Pressure addressed: query scattering — SQL is scattered, inconsistent, and untestable; Repository centralizes it behind a domain-facing boundary
answer_key.acceptable_variations: May describe specific method names differently
answer_key.full_credit: All three elements: what changes, what stays, and pressure named correctly
answer_key.partial_credit: Describes the refactor without naming the pressure, or names the pressure without describing the change

questions.prompt: You are designing the persistence layer for an e-commerce application with complex domain rules (multi-currency pricing, loyalty tiers, inventory reservations). Using Data Mapper, Repository, and Unit of Work together, explain: (1) what pressure each pattern addresses, (2) how they compose without overlap, and (3) what would break if you removed any one of them.
questions.guidance: A strong response maps each pattern to a distinct force, describes the information flow (domain → mapper → DB for writes; repo query → mapper reconstruction for reads; UoW wraps the whole write operation), and identifies the specific failure mode of removing each layer: removing Data Mapper couples domain to DB schema; removing Repository scatters query logic; removing UoW risks partial writes.

rubric.A: Correctly maps all three patterns to distinct pressures, describes composition with information flow, identifies specific failure mode for removing each
rubric.B: Correctly maps two of three, describes partial composition, identifies one or two removal consequences
rubric.C: Names all three without pressure mapping or composition description

M06.title: Assessment: Behavioral Patterns One by One

questions.question: What action-reification property of the Command pattern enables undo/redo functionality?

options.A: Commands are executed synchronously, so they can be cancelled
options.B: Each command is an object that encapsulates the action and optionally its inverse (undo logic), allowing it to be stored and reversed
options.C: Commands are immutable, so the original state is always recoverable
options.D: Commands log to a file automatically, enabling state reconstruction

questions.explanation: Reification means the action becomes a first-class object. That object can carry an execute() and undo() method. A history stack stores executed commands; undo pops and calls undo(). Synchronicity (A), immutability (C), and file logging (D) are not what enables this.

questions.question: A job processing system needs to retry failed jobs up to 3 times. Which Command property makes this possible without changing each job's business logic?

options.A: Commands are stateless, so retrying is always safe
options.B: The queue treats each job as a Command object and calls execute() again, managing retry count externally through the uniform interface
options.C: Command includes built-in retry logic via its base class
options.D: Commands automatically clone themselves for retry attempts

questions.explanation: Reification means the queue can hold, inspect, and re-execute any command through a uniform interface. The business logic inside execute() is unchanged — retry policy is managed externally by the queue. A, C, D are incorrect characterizations.

questions.question: What traceability cost does the Observer pattern introduce that direct method calls do not?

options.A: Observer requires a message broker that adds network latency
options.B: The control flow becomes implicit — a reader of the subject's code cannot see which handlers will run for an event
options.C: Observer forces all subscribers to run on the same thread
options.D: The subject must know all subscriber types to send the right event

questions.explanation: The traceability cost is implicit control flow. With Observer, firing an event triggers subscriber code that is invisible from the subject. Debugging requires knowing all registered observers. A describes messaging infrastructure. C is an implementation concern. D is wrong — Observer decouples subject from subscriber types.

questions.question: Claim C4 states behavioral patterns trade directness for coordination control. Which scenario best illustrates this tradeoff with Observer?

options.A: Observer removes the need for any interfaces between the subject and its subscribers
options.B: An Order fires an order_placed event; EmailService, InventoryService, and AnalyticsService respond independently — Order is simpler but a bug in InventoryService is invisible when reading Order's code
options.C: Observer is used to replace try/except blocks with event-based error handling
options.D: Observer allows multiple subjects to share a single subscriber

questions.explanation: This directly illustrates C4: directness is lost (implicit flow from Order to its subscribers) but coordination is controlled (Order doesn't need to enumerate or call each service). A, C, D mischaracterize Observer's mechanics.

questions.question: What condition-sprawl pressure justifies the State pattern over an if-elif chain?

options.A: The object has more than three states to track
options.B: State-dependent logic is spread across many methods and adding a new state requires modifying each one
options.C: The object must transition between states faster than an if-elif chain can execute
options.D: The application uses a database to persist state values

questions.explanation: State is justified when condition-heavy logic spreads across many methods and adding a state means modifying many places. If there are three states but only one method, an if-elif is simpler. A uses state count as a proxy — wrong. C and D describe performance and persistence, not the structural pressure.

questions.question: Distinguish State from Strategy: a Document changes behavior based on its status (Draft, Review, Published). An OrderProcessor selects a pricing algorithm (Standard, Premium, Bulk) per order. Which uses State and which uses Strategy?

options.A: Both use State; the pricing algorithm selection is just a special case
options.B: Document uses State (lifecycle transitions over time); OrderProcessor uses Strategy (algorithm selection at point of use)
options.C: Document uses Strategy; OrderProcessor uses State
options.D: Both use Strategy; the document status is just an algorithm selection

questions.explanation: Document transitions between states over its lifecycle (Draft → Review → Published) — that is State. OrderProcessor selects an algorithm per order at the point of use without lifecycle transitions — that is Strategy. C has them reversed. A and D collapse the distinction.

questions.question: A text editor uses Command for edit operations. When would adding Observer to the same system be justified alongside Command?

options.A: When there are more than 10 different command types
options.B: When multiple independent components (UI, undo history, collaborators) need to react to document changes without the document knowing each one
options.C: When Command objects need to communicate with each other
options.D: When undo operations must be confirmed by the user before executing

questions.explanation: Observer is justified when a document change must fan out to multiple independent consumers (UI rendering, undo stack, network sync) that shouldn't be coupled to the document. This is a distinct pressure from what Command addresses. A uses count as proxy. C and D are different concerns.

questions.question: Explain the difference between Command and a direct function call in terms of lifecycle control. Give one scenario where Command's lifecycle capabilities are essential.

required_points: Direct call: action executes immediately, no identity, no history, no deferral
Command: action is packaged as an object with identity — can be stored, queued, replayed, undone, serialized
Scenario where essential: undo/redo (must reverse past actions), job retry queue (must re-execute without rebuilding context), audit log (must record and replay specific actions)
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Any scenario that genuinely requires at least one of the lifecycle capabilities (defer, queue, replay, undo)
answer_key.full_credit: Correct distinction, lifecycle capabilities named, scenario where essential identified
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct distinction without lifecycle capabilities, or scenario without the distinction

questions.question: A Document class has if/elif status checks in 6 different methods. Sketch the State refactor: what classes are created, what disappears from Document, and what happens when a 7th state is added later?

required_points: Classes created: DraftState, ReviewState, PublishedState (one per state) each implementing the document's state-dependent interface
What disappears from Document: the if/elif chains in the 6 methods — Document delegates to self.state.method_name()
Adding a 7th state: create a new state class; Document and all existing state classes are unchanged — open/closed principle in practice
answer_key.acceptable_variations: State class names can vary; key elements are the delegation and the extensibility without modification
answer_key.full_credit: Names the classes, describes what is removed from Document, and explains extensibility correctly
answer_key.partial_credit: Describes the refactor without the extensibility consequence

questions.prompt: You are designing a collaborative document editor. Three behavioral pressures exist: (1) users can undo/redo edits, (2) multiple UI components must update when the document changes, (3) the document has three distinct editing modes (insert, read-only, commenting) with different behavior per mode. Using Command, Observer, and State, explain how these three patterns compose, what each owns, and what would break if you collapsed two of them into one.
questions.guidance: A strong response assigns Command → undo/redo history (action reification), Observer → reactive UI updates (fan-out notification), State → mode-based behavior (lifecycle-driven delegation). It explains they address different pressures so there is no overlap. The collapse test should show what breaks: e.g., merging Command and State creates objects that are both action records and mode controllers — conflated responsibilities.

rubric.A: Correctly assigns all three patterns to distinct pressures, describes composition (no overlap), performs the collapse test for at least two combinations
rubric.B: Correctly assigns two of three, describes partial composition, performs one collapse test
rubric.C: Names all three without pressure assignment or composition reasoning

questions.question: Transaction Script is sometimes criticized as 'bad OOP.' According to this module, when is Transaction Script the correct choice?

options.A: When the team does not know how to write domain objects
options.B: When domain complexity is modest, workflow is linear, and richer modeling would add unnecessary indirection
options.C: When the application has fewer than 500 users and doesn't need to scale
options.D: When a tight deadline makes Domain Model impractical

questions.explanation: Transaction Script is correct when the domain is genuinely simple — not as a fallback for skill gaps (A), scale concerns (C), or timeline pressure (D). Choosing it for the right reasons is honest simplicity; choosing it for the wrong reasons is technical debt.

questions.question: Contradiction 3 states Transaction Script and Domain Model are not competing universals but target different regions of the complexity curve. What determines which region a system is in?

options.A: The number of database tables in the schema
options.B: The density of business rules, shared invariants, and stateful interactions in the domain
options.C: Whether the team uses an ORM or raw SQL
options.D: The number of developers on the project

questions.explanation: The complexity curve is determined by domain richness — how many business rules exist, whether they are shared across workflows, and whether objects have meaningful stateful interactions. Table count (A), ORM choice (C), and team size (D) are not proxies for domain complexity.

questions.question: A loan approval system has 5 business rules checked in 4 different service methods. When does Domain Model become justified for this system?

options.A: When the team decides to adopt DDD
options.B: When the rules are shared across workflows and violating one would cause inconsistency that a single script cannot protect against
options.C: When the application is deployed to production and performance becomes critical
options.D: When the loan objects need to be serialized to JSON

questions.explanation: Domain Model becomes justified when rules are shared, must be enforced consistently, and putting them in a domain object is the only way to prevent duplication or violation. A is a team decision proxy. C and D are irrelevant to the domain complexity question.

questions.question: What is the Anemic Domain Model and how is it detected in code?

options.A: A domain model with too few domain objects; detected when there are fewer than 5 classes
options.B: Domain objects that are pure data containers with no behavior; detected when all business logic lives in service classes and objects only have getters/setters
options.C: A domain model that uses inheritance instead of composition; detected by deep class hierarchies
options.D: A domain model that doesn't use an ORM; detected when SQL appears in domain objects

questions.explanation: Anemic Domain Model: the object form exists but the behavioral substance is elsewhere. Objects are data bags; services do all the work. A conflates quantity with anemia. C is about design style. D describes Active Record, not anemia.

questions.question: A codebase has Order, Product, and Customer classes with only getters and setters. All discount, validation, and fulfillment logic lives in OrderService. Which two interpretations are both valid responses to this situation?

options.A: Add more classes, or refactor to use static methods
options.B: The domain is genuinely simple enough for Transaction Script (drop the class ceremony) OR domain complexity is real and behavior belongs on the objects (enrich the domain model)
options.C: Use Service Layer to absorb the remaining logic, or add a Registry for lookup
options.D: Switch from ORM to raw SQL, or add Unit of Work

questions.explanation: The Anemic Domain Model either means (1) the domain is really simple — commit to Transaction Script and remove the class ceremony — or (2) domain complexity is real — enrich the domain objects. Both are honest resolutions. The mismatch is having the object form without the behavioral substance.

questions.question: Domain Model adds complexity over Transaction Script. What specific pressure makes that complexity worth paying?

options.A: The team wants to practice OOP principles
options.B: Business rules are dense, shared across multiple workflows, and must be enforced as invariants on domain objects
options.C: The application has a microservices architecture that requires objects
options.D: Performance requirements demand that logic run inside database transactions

questions.explanation: Domain Model earns its complexity when rules must be centralized in objects to prevent duplication and ensure invariant enforcement. Team preference (A), architecture style (C), and performance (D) are not the relevant domain-complexity pressure.

questions.question: Distinguish the Anemic Domain Model from a deliberately simple Transaction Script design. What is the key difference?

options.A: Anemic Domain Model uses Java; Transaction Script uses Python
options.B: Anemic Domain Model is a mismatch — object form without behavioral substance at non-trivial domain complexity; Transaction Script is a deliberate choice at genuinely modest complexity
options.C: Transaction Script is only valid for batch processing; Anemic Domain Model is for web applications
options.D: Anemic Domain Model uses an ORM; Transaction Script uses raw SQL

questions.explanation: The distinction is intentionality and complexity fit. Transaction Script chosen because the domain is genuinely simple = correct. Classes-with-no-behavior when domain complexity is real = Anemic Domain Model mismatch. A, C, D apply irrelevant criteria.

questions.question: A billing system processes a single workflow: load invoice, apply flat 10% discount, write to DB. Should this use Transaction Script or Domain Model? Justify using Contradiction 3.

required_points: Transaction Script — the workflow is linear, rule is local (10% discount applies to everything), no shared invariants, no other workflows need this rule
Contradiction 3: TS and DM target different complexity regions — this is clearly in TS territory
Domain Model would add class overhead with nothing meaningful to encapsulate — the rule has no need for invariant protection or cross-workflow sharing
answer_key.acceptable_variations: May note that if more discount types or eligibility rules appeared, the answer could change
answer_key.full_credit: Correct recommendation, Contradiction 3 reasoning applied, explanation of why DM is overkill

questions.question: Describe what 'behavior belongs on the object' means in practice. Give one concrete example of a business rule that belongs inside a domain object rather than a service method.

required_points: 'Behavior belongs on the object' means the rule that validates or transforms an object's state should be a method on that object, not an external procedure
This protects invariants — no service can bypass the rule by skipping a service method call
Concrete example: Order.add_item(item) checks that the item is in stock before adding it — the invariant cannot be violated because the only way to add an item is through the method
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Any example where the domain object method enforces an invariant that would be duplicated or bypassable if it lived in a service
answer_key.full_credit: Correct principle, invariant protection explained, concrete example with a method that enforces a rule
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct example without the invariant protection explanation

questions.prompt: You are joining a codebase that has Order, Customer, and Product classes with only __init__ and property methods. All business rules live in OrderService, CustomerService, and ProductService. The business logic includes: discount eligibility based on customer tier and order total, out-of-stock prevention, and loyalty points calculation shared across 4 different workflows. Using the complexity curve from Module 7, evaluate whether this is (a) correctly using Transaction Script with unnecessary classes, or (b) an Anemic Domain Model that needs enrichment. Recommend a direction and explain what would change.
questions.guidance: A strong response identifies the shared-rule signal (loyalty points across 4 workflows, discount rules appearing in multiple services) as the indicator that Domain Model territory has been reached. It recommends enrichment: move the shared rules onto domain objects. It explains that if rules were all local and non-shared, removing the classes and committing to Transaction Script would be equally valid.

rubric.A: Correctly identifies the shared-rule signal, diagnoses Anemic Domain Model not Transaction Script, recommends enrichment with specific example of a rule that moves to a domain object, acknowledges the alternative (pure TS) would be valid if rules were local
rubric.B: Correct diagnosis, recommends enrichment, limited on the specific example or alternative consideration
rubric.C: Picks a direction without using the complexity curve or shared-rule signal as evidence

M08.title: Assessment: Criticism, Overuse, and Pattern Economics

questions.question: What is 'abstraction debt' and how does it differ from technical debt?

options.A: Abstraction debt is code written with poor algorithms; technical debt is poorly formatted code
options.B: Abstraction debt is indirection added before real pressure exists; technical debt is messy implementation that needs cleanup
options.C: Abstraction debt is incurred by junior developers; technical debt is created by experienced developers
options.D: Abstraction debt applies to patterns; technical debt applies to data models

questions.explanation: Abstraction debt comes from over-engineering — layers added speculatively. Technical debt comes from under-engineering — messy code that needs cleaning. Both create maintenance cost, but abstraction debt is created by too much structure, not too little.

questions.question: An abstraction 'pays rent' when it relieves a present cost. Which of the following abstractions is NOT paying rent?

options.A: A Repository added because SQL queries are scattered across 15 service methods and cause test failures
options.B: A Plugin architecture added 'in case we need a second payment provider someday'
options.C: A Unit of Work added because 3 objects are being saved in a transaction and partial writes have caused bugs
options.D: A Special Case added because null user checks appear in 10 different places

questions.explanation: B adds a Plugin for a speculative, non-existent future requirement — the pressure is imagined, not present. A, C, D all have concrete, present costs that justify the abstractions.

questions.question: Claim C5 states the central failure mode is applying patterns before real pressure exists. How do you operationally apply the 'rent test' before adding a pattern?

options.A: Check if the pattern is in the GoF book and commonly used in the industry
options.B: Name the specific cost the pattern relieves, confirm it is present now (not imagined), and verify the indirection cost is less than the relief it provides
options.C: Ask whether the team has used this pattern before in a similar codebase
options.D: Measure if the affected code has more than 100 lines

questions.explanation: The rent test has three operational steps: (1) name the cost, (2) confirm it's present, (3) confirm the relief exceeds the overhead. Industry use (A), team history (C), and line counts (D) are not operational pressure checks.

questions.question: Contradiction 1 states the choice between DI, Service Locator, and explicit passing is unresolved. What makes this particular debate impossible to resolve with a universal rule?

options.A: All three mechanisms produce identical code at the bytecode level
options.B: The right choice depends on context factors (team size, framework constraints, call-tree depth, testing discipline) that vary too much for a universal answer
options.C: The three mechanisms are equivalent in terms of testability and maintainability
options.D: Different programming languages favor different approaches, making a universal rule language-specific

questions.explanation: The threshold between acceptable SL and architectural debt depends on context — no study has measured it across the relevant variables. A, C are factually wrong. D is partially true but the research cites broader contextual factors, not just language.

questions.question: When does explicit dependency passing win over a DI container?

options.A: Always — explicit passing is always more readable than any container
options.B: When the call tree is shallow, dependency count is small, and the team values readability over framework leverage
options.C: When the codebase is too small to justify the learning curve of a DI container
options.D: When all dependencies are stateless services

questions.explanation: Explicit passing wins when the overhead of managing it manually is lower than the overhead of a DI container. Shallow call trees and few dependencies are the operational indicators. A makes it universal — wrong. C and D are plausible proxies but not the accurate characterization.

questions.question: Contradiction 2 states classic pattern forms may become obsolete while the underlying problem persists. Which modern Python example best illustrates this?

options.A: The Singleton pattern is replaced by module-level variables in Python
options.B: The Iterator pattern's ConcreteIterator class hierarchy is replaced by Python generators while the iteration problem persists
options.C: The Adapter pattern is replaced by Python's duck typing
options.D: The Repository pattern is replaced by Django's ORM QuerySet

questions.explanation: Python generators solve the iterator problem natively — the GoF class hierarchy is unnecessary, but the problem (lazily traversing a sequence) persists. A is debatable but Singleton isn't the clearest case. C and D overstate the replacement (duck typing doesn't eliminate Adapter; ORM doesn't fully replace Repository).

questions.question: YAGNI says 'you aren't gonna need it.' How should this principle be applied without becoming an excuse for no design?

options.A: Always start with the absolute minimum code and never add structure
options.B: Do not build abstractions for speculative future requirements, but do design cleanly for present requirements
options.C: Apply YAGNI only to features, not to code structure
options.D: Follow YAGNI until the codebase reaches 10,000 lines, then start adding patterns

questions.explanation: YAGNI targets speculation, not simplicity itself. Clean, present-requirement design and YAGNI are compatible. A collapses YAGNI into no-design. C incorrectly limits its scope. D applies an arbitrary size threshold.

questions.question: A tech lead adds a multi-tier caching abstraction 'because we'll need it when traffic grows.' Six months later, traffic has not grown and the cache layer has caused three production bugs. Apply the rent test retroactively: did this abstraction pay rent?

required_points: Rent test step 1: Name the cost the abstraction was meant to relieve — anticipated performance pressure from future traffic growth
Rent test step 2: Was the cost present at the time? No — traffic growth was speculative, not a present cost
Rent test step 3: Did the relief exceed the overhead? No — three production bugs and ongoing maintenance with no performance benefit
Verdict: The abstraction did not pay rent — it was added for a hypothetical future pressure, not a present one
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Can phrase the three steps differently; must reach the correct verdict with all three steps applied
answer_key.full_credit: All three rent test steps applied with correct verdict and reasoning
answer_key.partial_credit: Correct verdict without all three steps applied, or steps applied without clear verdict

questions.question: Explain why the dependency strategy debate (DI vs Service Locator vs explicit passing) is categorized as 'unresolved' rather than 'resolved in favor of DI.' What would a resolution require?

required_points: Unresolved because the right choice depends on contextual variables that no empirical study has measured: team size, framework constraints, call-tree depth, testing discipline
Fowler and Nystrom represent different views — neither is empirically falsified
Resolution would require: controlled studies measuring maintainability, bug rate, or onboarding time under each approach at matched complexity levels
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Can phrase contextual variables differently; key is the missing empirical measurement
answer_key.full_credit: Names the contextual variables, explains why they prevent a universal rule, describes what evidence would resolve it
answer_key.partial_credit: Correctly says unresolved without explaining contextual factors or resolution criteria

questions.prompt: You are reviewing a codebase before merging a PR. The PR adds: a Plugin architecture (no second implementation exists), a Repository (SQL is scattered in 8 service methods), a Unit of Work (3 objects are saved in one transaction and have caused partial write bugs), and an Abstract Factory (only one product family exists with no second planned). Using the rent test and Claim C5, write a structured code review comment that accepts some additions and rejects others, with justification for each.
questions.guidance: A strong response accepts Repository (scattered SQL = present cost) and Unit of Work (partial write bugs = present cost), rejects Plugin (speculative, no second implementation) and Abstract Factory (speculative, no second product family). Each verdict should cite the rent test steps: name the cost, confirm it's present, confirm the relief exceeds overhead.

rubric.A: Correct verdicts on all four, rent test applied explicitly to each, C5 referenced as the governing principle
rubric.B: Correct verdicts on three of four, rent test partially applied
rubric.C: Correct verdicts without rent test reasoning, or mixed verdicts

questions.question: In the enterprise pattern stack, which pattern owns the orchestration pressure and which owns the invariant-enforcement pressure?

options.A: Repository owns orchestration; Unit of Work owns invariants
options.B: Service Layer owns orchestration; Domain Model owns invariants
options.C: Data Mapper owns orchestration; Repository owns invariants
options.D: Unit of Work owns orchestration; Service Layer owns invariants

questions.explanation: Service Layer = use case coordination (orchestration). Domain Model = business rules and invariants. Repository and Data Mapper address query abstraction and object-relational separation respectively. UoW handles transaction scope.

questions.question: What is 'responsibility layering' and how does it prevent pattern stacking?

options.A: Organizing code by technical layer (controller, service, model) rather than by business domain
options.B: Assigning one pattern per one design pressure so no two patterns own the same concern
options.C: Limiting each layer of the architecture to a maximum number of classes
options.D: Using inheritance to layer behavior across parent and child classes

questions.explanation: Responsibility layering means each pattern addresses a distinct force with no overlap. It prevents stacking because the test is 'which pressure does this pattern own?' — if another pattern already owns that pressure, one of them is redundant. A, C, D describe different concepts.

questions.question: A system uses Service Layer + Repository + Unit of Work, but domain objects have no behavior — all rules are in the service. What is missing and what symptom indicates it?

options.A: Data Mapper is missing; symptom is SQL appearing in domain objects
options.B: Domain Model is hollow; symptom is business rules duplicated across service methods and no invariant enforcement on domain objects
options.C: Unit of Work is unnecessary; symptom is too many explicit save() calls
options.D: Observer is missing; symptom is UI not updating after service calls

questions.explanation: A structural enterprise stack without Domain Model richness is an Anemic Domain Model pattern — objects exist but hold no behavior. The symptom is rule duplication across services and no invariant protection. A, C, D describe different missing elements.

questions.question: In an editor/workflow composition (Command, Observer, State), which pattern owns the 'user can undo their last 10 actions' requirement?

options.A: Observer — by storing state snapshots on each notification
options.B: State — by tracking historical states in the state machine
options.C: Command — by storing executed command objects in a history stack and calling their undo() methods
options.D: A combination of all three is required

questions.explanation: Undo/redo is Command's core lifecycle feature — execute() creates the action, undo() reverses it, a history stack manages the sequence. Observer handles notification fan-out; State handles mode transitions. No combination is needed for undo specifically.

questions.question: How do Command and Observer interact in a text editor without overlapping responsibilities?

options.A: They don't interact — they address completely independent parts of the system
options.B: Command executes the edit action (and makes it undoable); after execution, Observer notifies all components that the document has changed
